comments.text: Note that the same simple double-counting lower bound from #104 gives $h(n) \ge (n - 2)/2$. For an upper bound, I'll sketch below how to get $h(n) \le n^2\exp\left(O(\sqrt{\log{n}})\right)$. There is unfortunately still quite a large gap between these bounds. We use the following standard projection observation. Let \(S\subset\mathbb R^D\) be finite and have no three collinear points. A generic linear projection \(\pi:S\to\mathbb R^2\) is injective and gives a planar point set with no three collinear and no four concyclic. Moreover, if two ordered triples in \(S\) have the same translation class, i.e.\[ (b-a,c-a)=(b'-a',c'-a'), \]then their projected triples are translates of one another, and hence have the same circumradius. Thus, writing\[ \tau(S):=\#\{(b-a,c-a):a,b,c\in S\}, \]we have\[ h(n)\le \tau(S) \]whenever \(|S|\ge n\). Let \(B_m\) be the Euclidean unit ball in \(\mathbb R^m\), and put\[ A_{m,R}:=\mathbb Z^m\cap R B_m. \]Define the paraboloid lift\[ S_{m,R}:=\{(x,\|x\|^2):x\in A_{m,R}\} \subset\mathbb R^{m+1}. \]This set has no three collinear points, since the function \(x\mapsto \|x\|^2\) is strictly convex on every affine line. Let\[ N:=|S_{m,R}|=|A_{m,R}|. \]for \(R=\exp(\Theta(m))\), so that\[ N=R^m\operatorname{vol}(B_m)\exp(o(m)). \]We now count translation classes of ordered triples in \(S_{m,R}\). A triple is of the form\[ (x,\|x\|^2),\qquad (x+u,\|x+u\|^2),\qquad (x+v,\|x+v\|^2), \]where\[ x,x+u,x+v\in A_{m,R}. \]Its translation class is determined by\[ u,\quad v,\quad \Delta_u:=\|x+u\|^2-\|x\|^2,\quad \Delta_v:=\|x+v\|^2-\|x\|^2. \]Since all squared norms involved lie in \([0,R^2]\), each of \(\Delta_u,\Delta_v\) has only \(O(R^2)\) possible values. Thus the two lifted coordinate differences contribute \(O(R^4)\) possibilities. It remains to count the possible base pairs \((u,v)\). Define\[ D_m:= \{(u,v)\in\mathbb R^m\times\mathbb R^m: \exists y\in\mathbb R^m\text{ with }y,y+u,y+v\in B_m\}. \]Then the possible integer pairs \((u,v)\) are contained in\[ \mathbb Z^{2m}\cap R D_m. \]The body \(D_m\) is convex, contains the Euclidean unit ball in \(\mathbb R^{2m}\), and is contained in a bounded Euclidean ball. Therefore\[ |\mathbb Z^{2m}\cap R D_m| \le (R+O(\sqrt m))^{2m}\operatorname{vol}(D_m) = R^{2m}\operatorname{vol}(D_m)\exp(o(m)). \]Letting $M = 3\sqrt{3}/2$ it's not hard to show that\[ \operatorname{vol}(D_m) \le \operatorname{vol}(B_m)^2 M^m\exp(o(m)). \]It follows that\[ |\mathbb Z^{2m}\cap R D_m| \le R^{2m}\operatorname{vol}(B_m)^2 M^m\exp(o(m)). \]Since\[ N=R^m\operatorname{vol}(B_m)\exp(o(m)), \]we get\[ |\mathbb Z^{2m}\cap R D_m| \le N^2 M^m\exp(o(m)). \]Including the \(O(R^4)\) possibilities for the two lifted-coordinate differences,\[ \tau(S_{m,R}) \le N^2 M^m R^4\exp(o(m)). \]Now choose \(m\) and \(R\) so that \(N\ge n\) and \(N=n\exp(o(m))\). Since\[ N=R^m\operatorname{vol}(B_m)\exp(o(m)), \]we have\[ \log R = \frac{\log n}{m}+O(\log m). \]Thus\[ \log\frac{h(n)}{n^2} \le m\log M+4\log R+o(m) = m\log M+\frac{4\log n}{m}+o(\sqrt{\log n}). \]Optimizing by taking\[ m\sim 2\sqrt{\frac{\log n}{\log M}}, \]we obtain\[ \log\frac{h(n)}{n^2} \le 4\sqrt{\log M}\sqrt{\log n} + o(\sqrt{\log n}). \]as desired. AI Acknowledgement: While the projection argument and use of paraboloid lifting to avoid collinearity was developed without AI assistance, GPT-5.5 was used to optimize the constant in the exponent and write the final comment.
